title: Make the AI understand your audience

spec.title: Make the AI understand your audience
spec.notes_for_generator: Rewrite a paragraph for two different audiences. Teaches context-setting — the concept that AI output quality changes dramatically when you specify who it's for. Full prompt template provided with brackets for the learner to fill in.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 2: Your audience changes everything

The same information, written for two different people, should read like two completely different documents. Most people know this. Most people forget to tell the AI.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
🔄 TRANSLATION | 🟢 WARM-UP | ⏱ 10–15 min

THE BRIEF:
You've written a short paragraph explaining something about your work — a project update, a product feature, a process change. You need to share it with two very different audiences: one who knows the topic inside out, and one who doesn't. Same core message. Two completely different versions. The AI can do this — but only if you tell it who it's talking to.

YOUR REPS:

1. Find a paragraph from your work — a recent email, a report, a slide. Keep it to 3–6 sentences. Then send this prompt, filling in the brackets:

*"Rewrite the following paragraph for [AUDIENCE 1 — e.g. 'a senior finance director with no technical background']. Keep the core message the same but adjust the language, tone, and level of detail to suit them. Here is the paragraph: [PASTE YOUR PARAGRAPH]"*

Read what comes back. Don't judge it yet — just notice what changed.

2. Now run the same prompt again, swapping in a second audience — someone very different from the first. Try to pick someone where the gap is obvious: a specialist vs. a generalist, a client vs. a colleague, a new joiner vs. a department head.

Compare both outputs side by side. What did the AI actually change — the vocabulary, the structure, the assumed knowledge? What stayed the same that probably shouldn't have?

3. Pick the version that landed less well and send a follow-up prompt. Tell the AI specifically what it got wrong: *"The tone is still too technical — my reader wouldn't know what [specific term] means. Try again, this time avoiding all jargon and leading with why it matters to them."*

See if giving a concrete correction gets you closer than the original instruction did.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• A reader in each audience group would recognise the version written for them — the language, detail level, and framing should feel right for who they are
• At least one version has noticeably changed the structure or order of information, not just swapped out words
• Your follow-up prompt in Rep 3 is more specific than "make it better" — it names exactly what to fix

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Specifying your audience is one of the highest-leverage habits you can build with AI. The model isn't guessing who will read the output — it's responding to whatever context you provide. Vague context produces vague adaptation. When you name a real person (their role, their knowledge level, what they care about), you're not just setting a style guide — you're telling the AI what to prioritise and what to leave out. This applies any time you're using AI to communicate: emails, reports, presentations, pitches. The question "who is this for?" should become as automatic as hitting send.

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
